No Poverty.reason: The course's core values of 'social co-goodness' and 'social concern,' along with case studies on 'rural education,' align with supporting vulnerable populations and reducing poverty (Target 1.b).

Zero Hunger.reason: Explicit mentions of 'health care and food agriculture' and 'organic agriculture' directly relate to sustainable food production systems (Target 2.4).

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course explicitly covers 'health care and food agriculture,' 'Finnish elderly care,' and 'vaccines,' directly promoting well-being and health services (Targets 3.4, 3.8).

Quality Education.reason: As an educational course, it explicitly features 'Education learning movement,' 'rural education,' and 'digital teaching platform,' directly contributing to quality education for sustainable development (Target 4.7).

Gender Equality.reason: No explicit evidence or discussion related to gender equality, women's empowerment, or related issues was found in the course syllabus.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit evidence or discussion related to clean water, sanitation, or related issues was found in the course syllabus.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: Explicit discussions on 'zero-carbon future,' 'net-zero,' and 'green sustainable strategies' directly relate to increasing clean energy and efficiency (Targets 7.2, 7.3), indicating a stronger connection than a mere inference.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course's focus on 'corporate innovation,' 'local revitalization,' and 'humanities entrepreneurs,' with case studies like 'local tourism life,' directly promotes entrepreneurship and sustainable economic growth (Targets 8.2, 8.3, 8.9).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course title 'Humanities Innovation & Sustainability Development' and explicit themes like 'corporate innovation,' 'technological innovation,' and 'industry innovation' make this a central and direct focus (Targets 9.2, 9.4, 9.5).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: Core values like 'social co-goodness' and initiatives such as 'rural education' and 'local revitalization' explicitly address social inclusion and reducing disparities (Targets 10.2, 10.3).

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: Strong emphasis on 'local revitalization,' 'regional (sustainable) innovation,' and numerous case studies like 'Zhushan town' and cultural parks directly support sustainable human settlements and cultural heritage (Targets 11.3, 11.4, 11.7).

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: Dedicated lectures on 'Corporate Sustainable Development Practices,' 'ESG sustainability report practical analysis,' and 'green sustainable strategies' explicitly encourage sustainable business practices (Target 12.6).

Climate Action.reason: A dedicated lecture covers 'carbon inventory, carbon neutrality, net-zero' and 'carbon reduction adaptation plans,' explicitly integrating climate change measures into business strategies (Targets 13.2, 13.3).

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit evidence or discussion related to marine ecosystems, ocean health, or related issues was found in the course syllabus.

Life on Land.reason: The course's emphasis on 'autonomous symbiosis of the entire ecosystem' and a lecture on 'Environmental Protection (E)' infers a connection to terrestrial ecosystem conservation (Target 15.1).

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: Lectures on 'Corporate Governance (G)' and 'corporate sustainable development regulations,' alongside 'institutional (social) innovation,' directly address developing effective and accountable institutions (Target 16.6).

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The core concept of 'autonomous symbiosis and complementary co-creation of the ecosystem' and the final project's requirement to 'combine corporate resources with private friends' explicitly promote multi-stakeholder partnerships (Targets 17.16, 17.17).
